text: Syboll. Shall we parley?” Rickard tensed, knuckles whitening in their grip on the chair. He moved around front, steepled his fingers on the table on either side of the stack of parchment, and said, “Let’s talk.” Ergant sidled around his own chair, the two facing each other over the length of the dark oak surface. Not mirrors of each other—Rickard was broader of shoulder, less refined, harsher and blunt—but equals. They sat as one, neither dropping their gaze from the other. Aurel shifted forward to Rickard’s right, yet a step behind, and eyed the woman to Ergant’s left. A mirrorwright as well—she must be—but not one he knew. Not trained at the Arcanum. Her complexion was from the Eastern Isles and white paint dotted her forehead and the skin near her eyes, with swirls of darker tattoos along her throat. The mirrorwright gave him a faint nod of acknowledgment. “I assume you’ve come to apologize for the blatant attacks by your military on the islands along our border—” “ I have come to apologize?” Rickard interrupted, his voice like gravel. “It is you who needs to apologize. Your incursions into Tregard territory— my territory —will no longer be tolerated.” “Incursions? I don’t know of any…incursions.” Rickard slammed a palm flat against the table, the ink, candle, and container of sand jumping with a rattle, but before he could speak, a fifth figure emerged from one of the mirrors midway down the table to their left. Their hands were hidden inside the sleeves of their gray robes, the hood pulled down to shield their face. When they spoke, their voice was genderless. “Rickard Syboll, you have requested arbitration of the Forum. Ergant Tyroll, you have accepted. This chamber has been provided for you, along with food and wine, for as long as it takes for you to reach a mutual agreement so that the Seven Isles can remain at peace.” As they spoke, two more robed figures emerged from mirrors on either end of the table and set decanters of wine, glasses, and trays of assorted fruits, breads, and cheeses on the table, then turned and vanished back through the mirrors. “There will be no blood shed in this room and no one shall leave until an agreement has been reached between the two parties. All are equals here. Is it so agreed?” Rickard stood, leaning toward Ergant in challenge. “It is agreed.” Ergant frowned, his arrogance faltering, but he said, “Agreed.” The robed figure bowed. “So it is agreed.” Then he turned and slid into the mirror behind him. Aurel felt a pulse of power shiver through his flesh as all of the flames in the room guttered as if in a breeze. He twisted and reached with one hand toward the mirror that led to Tregard, to home, felt the prickling of the magic against his palm as before, but when he muttered the incantation and pressed his hand forward it met the cold surface of the glass. He faced Rickard. “They’ve sealed the room.” “Good,” the king said, swiveling toward Ergant. “Then we can finally get justice for what has been done.” “Justice?” Ergant asked. “It is Elleron that seeks justice here, for what you have done.” Rickard snorted. “And what do you believe I have done?” Ergant shifted in his seat, rested his elbows on the table, and clasped his hands together. “Does that mean you are ready to proceed?” Rickard hesitated, then sank back into his chair. “I am.” Ergant reached for his quill, tested the nib, then dipped it in the ink. Hand poised over the first sheet of parchment, he glanced up at Rickard. “Then let us list our grievances.” Snatching up his own quill, Rickard began to write. The scritch-scratch of writing filled the hall, the sound grating in Aurel’s bones. Attempting to ignore it, and the lower thrum of his own anger wadded up deep inside, he glared at the unknown mirrorwright, at Ergant, and focused on his breathing. He’d come as an advisor—and because he was Rickard’s only mirrorwright—not because of Elean and the rest of her family; not because of what had been done to their village of Istabol because of the orders of this madman. Even as he thought it, his hands closed into fists. He tore his gaze away from Ergant and faced the wall of mirrors behind him. His reflection caught his gaze, but almost immediately he turned away. He did not want to see his haggard face, the creases of grief that had been etched around his eyes and mouth, especially when he knew there were mirrors here that would reveal more than mere reality, that would skew it, exaggerate it, enhance it. Not all mirrors were traveling portals. Some were portals to the soul. Eyes closed, he seized control of his breath, forced the tension in his shoulders to ease. His fingers unclenched, one hand reaching into a pocket to grip the damning cloth folded up inside. His thumb caressed the embroidered emblem and his resolve hardened. He returned to his position as Rickard finished writing. The king paused, quill trembling, then he set it aside and blew gently on the parchment. Sand came next, sprinkled so as to absorb any blobs of ink that remained, parchment tapped on its edge to clean it off. Then he sat back, eyes on Ergant as the King of Elleron continued scribbling. Aurel glanced over Rickard’s shoulder at the list of grievances, frowning at how trivial some of them sounded. An instance where Ergant had insulted Rickard’s lineage at a formal gala. A personal slight at the most recent regatta, the competition this year between the isles particular fierce. A few trading contracts that had never been fulfilled. But then he reached the more serious infractions, those that had driven them to the arbitration: the attacks on Tregard’s trading ships along the Elleron border seas; the ships that had simply vanished, all hands and cargo presumed lost; the
